symptom: Tech is staring at a superheat reading on a system with a thermostatic expansion valve and trying to use it to diagnose refrigerant charge. The superheat number is dancing because the TXV is doing its job — modulating to hold a target superheat regardless of charge.
diagnosis: Wrong measurement for the metering device. On a TXV system, superheat is held nearly constant by the valve, so it cannot be used to judge charge. The diagnostic value lives in subcooling at the liquid line — that's the variable that moves with charge on a TXV.
procedure: Confirm the metering device by inspecting the indoor coil — TXV has a sensing bulb clamped to the suction line near the coil outlet.
Connect to the liquid-line schrader; record liquid-line pressure and saturation temperature.
Measure liquid-line temperature with a clamp-on probe a few inches downstream of the condenser.
Calculate subcooling = saturation temperature − liquid-line temperature.
Compare to the equipment label or installation manual (often 8–12°F).
Low subcooling → suspect undercharge or restriction. High subcooling → suspect overcharge.
safety_notes: Don't add refrigerant based on superheat on a TXV — it will look like the system is undercharged forever and you'll end up overcharged.
Verify the TXV sensing bulb is correctly clamped and insulated before blaming the valve itself.
A blocked TXV mimics undercharge; check the liquid-line filter-drier before condemning the valve.
